remedies.title: Gentle 12-Hour Overnight Fast
instructions: Finish dinner at a regular time, e.g. 7 pm.
Take only water or plain herbal tea until breakfast 12 hours later, e.g. 7 am.
Keep the window consistent for a few weeks before judging the effect.
Break the fast with a normal, unhurried breakfast.
remedies.origin: The traditional rhythm of 'break-fast' itself — most pre-industrial households simply did not eat after dark.
remedies.disclaimer: Do not fast if you are pregnant, breastfeeding, diabetic, underweight, have a history of disordered eating, or take medication with food — consult your doctor first.

remedies.title: 16:8 Time-Restricted Eating

ingredients.name: water, unsweetened tea, or black coffee

instructions: Choose an 8-hour eating window that fits your life, e.g. 11 am to 7 pm.
Eat normal, nourishing meals inside the window — this is not a licence to overeat or a reason to undereat.
Outside the window take only water, unsweetened tea, or black coffee.
Start with 12 hours and lengthen gradually over a couple of weeks.
Stop if you feel dizzy, weak, or unwell, and eat.
remedies.origin: A modern framing of eating rhythms found in monastic and agrarian traditions worldwide.
remedies.disclaimer: Not suitable for pregnant or breastfeeding women, children, diabetics on medication, or anyone with a history of disordered eating. Medical guidance is essential if you take regular medication.

remedies.title: Dawn-to-Dusk Fasting Rhythm (Ramadan-style)

ingredients.name: pre-dawn meal (suhoor): slow-release foods like oats, dates, eggs
sunset meal (iftar): water and dates first, then a balanced meal

instructions: Eat a substantial, slow-release meal before dawn.
Take no food or drink during daylight hours.
Break the fast at sunset gently — traditionally with water and dates — before the main meal.
Prioritise hydration and unprocessed food between sunset and dawn.
Practise for a defined period, ideally alongside family or community.
remedies.origin: The daylight fast of Ramadan, practised for over 1,400 years across the Muslim world.
remedies.disclaimer: Dry fasting (no water) is demanding: the sick, pregnant, travelling, elderly and children are traditionally exempt. Anyone with a medical condition should consult a doctor — religious authorities themselves mandate these exemptions.
